description.en: Unlock __str__, __repr__, __len__, __eq__ and more — the dunder methods that make your objects behave like built-in Python types.

story_variants.en:
## The Hidden Operators

When you write `len([1, 2, 3])`, Python secretly calls `list.__len__([1, 2, 3])`. Every operator and built-in function has a **magic method** (dunder = double-underscore) behind it.

Define these on your own class to make it behave like a native Python type.

### Key Magic Methods

| Method | Triggered by | Use |
|--------|-------------|-----|
| `__init__` | `MyClass()` | constructor |
| `__str__` | `str(obj)`, `print()` | user-friendly string |
| `__repr__` | `repr(obj)`, REPL | developer string |
| `__len__` | `len(obj)` | length |
| `__eq__` | `obj1 == obj2` | equality |
| `__lt__` | `obj1 < obj2` | less-than |
| `__add__` | `obj1 + obj2` | addition |
| `__contains__` | `x in obj` | membership |

> **Rule of thumb:** Always define `__repr__` — it helps debugging. Define `__str__` for user-facing display. They can differ.

### Operator Overloading
By defining `__add__`, `__mul__` etc., you can make `Vector(1,2) + Vector(3,4)` return `Vector(4,6)`.

left.label: You write
left.code:
print(v1)
v1 + v2
v1 == v2
len(v1)

instruction.en: Create a class 'Book' with title and pages. Add __str__ returning 'Title (N pages)' and __lt__ comparing by page count.
Create a class 'Bag' that wraps a list. Add __len__ and __contains__ so len() and 'in' work on it.

hints.en: def __contains__(self, item): return item in self.items
